<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check white marker positions
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== White marker positions ===")
white_cells = []
for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):
    for c, ch in enumerate(line):
        if ch == 'W':
            white_cells.append((r, c))

print(f"White cells: {white_cells}")

# Group by row
from collections import defaultdict
by_row = defaultdict(list)
for r, c in white_cells:
    by_row[r].append(c)

for r in sorted(by_row.keys()):
    print(f"  Row {r}: cols {by_row[r]}")

# Let me check the initial state from history to see where markers started
if history:
    init_frame = history[0].frame
    init_ascii = init_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    print("\n=== Initial white marker positions ===")
    init_white = []
    for r, line in enumerate(init_ascii):
        for c, ch in enumerate(line):
            if ch == 'W':
                init_white.append((r, c))
    by_row_init = defaultdict(list)
    for r, c in init_white:
        by_row_init[r].append(c)
    for r in sorted(by_row_init.keys()):
        print(f"  Row {r}: cols {by_row_init[r]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

      "arguments": "{\"code\": \"# Check when markers moved\\n# Look at the first few transitions\\nfor i, trans in enumerate(transitions[:5]):\\n    before_ascii = trans.before_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\n    after_ascii = trans.after_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\n    \\n    def get_white_centers(ascii_lines):\\n        white_cells = []\\n        for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):\\n            for c, ch in enumerate(line):\\n                if ch == 'W':\\n                    white_cells.append((r, c))\\n        return white_cells\\n    \\n    before_w = get_white_centers(before_ascii)\\n    after_w = get_white_centers(after_ascii)\\n    \\n    if before_w != after_w:\\n        print(f\\\"Transition {i} ({trans.action}): markers moved\\\")\\n        print(f\\\"  Before: {before_w[:3]}...\\\")\\n        print(f\\\"  After: {after_w[:3]}...\\\")\\n    else:\\n        print(f\\\"Transition {i} ({trans.action}): markers stayed\\\")\\n\\n# Now let me check what the cursor position is\\n# The markers define which column is selected\\nascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\nwhite_cells = []\\nfor r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):\\n    for c, ch in enumerate(line):\\n        if ch == 'W':\\n            white_cells.append((r, c))\\n\\n# Find the column range\\ncols = [c for r, c in white_cells]\\nmin_col, max_col = min(cols), max(cols)\\nprint(f\\\"\\\\nCurrent marker cols: {min_col} to {max_col}\\\")\\n# Position 0 starts at col 14, each position is 7 cols wide\\n# So position = (col - 14) // 7\\n# But markers span 5 cols centered on the symbol\\npos = (min_col - 14 + 2) // 7\\nprint(f\\\"Current position: {pos}\\\")\\n\"}"

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check when markers moved
# Look at the first few transitions
for i, trans in enumerate(transitions[:5]):
    before_ascii = trans.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    after_ascii = trans.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    
    def get_white_centers(ascii_lines):
        white_cells = []
        for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):
            for c, ch in enumerate(line):
                if ch == 'W':
                    white_cells.append((r, c))
        return white_cells
    
    before_w = get_white_centers(before_ascii)
    after_w = get_white_centers(after_ascii)
    
    if before_w != after_w:
        print(f"Transition {i} ({trans.action}): markers moved")
        print(f"  Before: {before_w[:3]}...")
        print(f"  After: {after_w[:3]}...")
    else:
        print(f"Transition {i} ({trans.action}): markers stayed")

# Now let me check what the cursor position is
# The markers define which column is selected
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
white_cells = []
for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines):
    for c, ch in enumerate(line):
        if ch == 'W':
            white_cells.append((r, c))

# Find the column range
cols = [c for r, c in white_cells]
min_col, max_col = min(cols), max(cols)
print(f"\nCurrent marker cols: {min_col} to {max_col}")
# Position 0 starts at col 14, each position is 7 cols wide
# So position = (col - 14) // 7
# But markers span 5 cols centered on the symbol
pos = (min_col - 14 + 2) // 7
print(f"Current position: {pos}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check: can each pink position be cycled to match its sky symbol?
# First, get current sky and pink symbols
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get all sky and pink symbols
sky_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]
pink_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]

print("=== Current symbols ===")
for i in range(5):
    print(f"Pos {i}: Sky={' | '.join(sky_syms[i])}")
    print(f"       Pink={' | '.join(pink_syms[i])}")
    match = "MATCH" if sky_syms[i] == pink_syms[i] else ""
    print(f"       {match}\n")

# Check which positions already match
for i in range(5):
    if sky_syms[i] == pink_syms[i]:
        print(f"Position {i}: Sky and Pink already match!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

--- analysis_step=9 | action=57 | 13:55:21 | tool-agent ---
[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

The pink bar has been modified from the initial state. Let me think about what the goal could be.
Maybe the goal is to make each pink symbol match the corresponding sky symbol. I need to:

2. Check if any state matches the sky symbol at that position
3. If so, that's the target state

```
for target_pos in range(5):
    if target_pos > 0:
        action(['RIGHT'] * target_pos)
```
This should have moved the cursor to each position. But wait, the cursor was at position 0 when I started, and I moved RIGHT to positions 1, 2, 3, 4. But after each position, I cycled through 6 DOWN presses. The cursor should have stayed at each position during the DOWN presses.
Hmm, but positions 3 and 4 showed no change. Let me verify this by going to position 3 and pressing DOWN to see if the symbol changes.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me verify: go to position 3 and check if DOWN changes anything
# First, move to position 3
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Check white markers
white_cells = [(r, c) for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines) for c, ch in enumerate(line) if ch == 'W']
cols = [c for r, c in white_cells]
print(f"Marker cols: {min(cols)} to {max(cols)}")

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get current pink symbol at position 3
pink3_before = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)
print(f"Pink 3 before: {' | '.join(pink3_before)}")

# Press DOWN and check
action(['DOWN'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
pink3_after = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)
print(f"Pink 3 after 1 DOWN: {' | '.join(pink3_after)}")

# Press DOWN again
action(['DOWN'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
pink3_after2 = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)
print(f"Pink 3 after 2 DOWN: {' | '.join(pink3_after2)}")

# Check if changed
print(f"\nChanged after 1 DOWN: {pink3_before != pink3_after}")
print(f"Changed after 2 DOWN: {pink3_after != pink3_after2}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

pos -> number of DOWN presses needed\\n\\nfor pos in range(5):\\n    # Move to position\\n    if pos > 0:\\n        action(['RIGHT'] * pos)\\n    ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\n    \\n    sky_target = sky_syms[pos]\\n    c0 = 14 + pos * 7\\n    \\n    # Record current pink state as state 0\\n    pink_states = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, c0)]\\n    \\n    # Cycle through 6 more DOWN presses\\n    for d in range(6):\\n        action(['DOWN'])\\n        ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\\\\n')\\n        pink_states.append(get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, c0))\\n    \\n    # Find which state matches sky\\n    match_found = False\\n    for state_idx, state in enumerate(pink_states):\\n        if state == sky_target:\\n            match_plan[pos] = state_idx\\n            print(f\\\"Position {pos}: Sky matches Pink state {state_idx}\\\")\\n            match_found = True\\n            break\\n    \\n    if not match_found:\\n        print(f\\\"Position {pos}: NO MATCH FOUND\\\")\\n        print(f\\\"  Sky: {' | '.join(sky_target)}\\\")\\n        for i, s in enumerate(pink_states):\\n            print(f\\\"  Pink {i}: {' | '.join(s)}\\\")\\n\\nprint(f\\\"\\\\n=== Match plan ===\\\")\\nprint(match_plan)\\n\"}"

[THINKING]
Good, position 3 DOES change with DOWN. My earlier exploration was wrong - the symbols did change, I just misread the output. The issue was that my exploration code was cycling through positions but the cursor position tracking was off.
Now I know:
- LEFT/RIGHT moves the cursor between positions 0-4
- UP/DOWN cycles the pink symbol at the current cursor position
- Each position has a cycle of 7 states
- The goal is likely to make each pink symbol match its corresponding sky symbol
Let me now systematically explore all positions and find which DOWN state makes each pink match its sky symbol. I need to:
1. For each position 0-4:
   a. Get the sky symbol (target)
   b. Cycle through all 7 pink states
   c. Find which state matches the sky symbol
2. Then set each position to its matching state
Let me do this systematically, starting fresh from position 0.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Systematically find the matching state for each position
# First, go to position 0
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get all sky symbols (they don't change)
sky_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]

# For each position, find the DOWN state that matches sky
match_plan = {}  # pos -> number of DOWN presses needed

for pos in range(5):
    # Move to position
    if pos > 0:
        action(['RIGHT'] * pos)
    ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    
    sky_target = sky_syms[pos]
    c0 = 14 + pos * 7
    
    # Record current pink state as state 0
    pink_states = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, c0)]
    
    # Cycle through 6 more DOWN presses
    for d in range(6):
        action(['DOWN'])
        ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
        pink_states.append(get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, c0))
    
    # Find which state matches sky
    match_found = False
    for state_idx, state in enumerate(pink_states):
        if state == sky_target:
            match_plan[pos] = state_idx
            print(f"Position {pos}: Sky matches Pink state {state_idx}")
            match_found = True
            break
    
    if not match_found:
        print(f"Position {pos}: NO MATCH FOUND")
        print(f"  Sky: {' | '.join(sky_target)}")
        for i, s in enumerate(pink_states):
            print(f"  Pink {i}: {' | '.join(s)}")

print(f"\n=== Match plan ===")
print(match_plan)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state and continue exploration more efficiently
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Check which position we're at
white_cells = [(r, c) for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines) for c, ch in enumerate(line) if ch == 'W']
cols = [c for r, c in white_cells]
current_pos = (min(cols) - 14 + 2) // 7
print(f"Current position: {current_pos}")

# Get all current symbols
sky_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]
pink_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]

print("\n=== Current state ===")
for i in range(5):
    match = "MATCH" if sky_syms[i] == pink_syms[i] else ""
    print(f"Pos {i}: Sky={' | '.join(sky_syms[i])}")
    print(f"       Pink={' | '.join(pink_syms[i])} {match}")

# Check which positions already match
for i in range(5):
    if sky_syms[i] == pink_syms[i]:
        print(f"\nPosition {i} already matches!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I'm at position 3. Let me cycle through all states and find matches
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

sky3 = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + 3 * 7)
print(f"Sky 3: {' | '.join(sky3)}")

# Current pink state is state 0 for position 3
pink_states = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)]
print(f"Pink 3 state 0: {' | '.join(pink_states[0])}")

# Cycle through 6 more states
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

for d in range(1, 7):
    pink_states.append(get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7))
    print(f"Pink 3 state {d}: {' | '.join(pink_states[d])}")
    if d < 6:
        action(['DOWN'])
        ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Check for matches
print(f"\n=== Position 3 matches ===")
for i, s in enumerate(pink_states):
    if s == sky3:
        print(f"State {i} matches Sky 3!")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

Length = {i}\\\")\\n        break\\n    seen.add(pink)\\n\"}"

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Re-explore position 3 carefully, one DOWN at a time
# First check current position
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
white_cells = [(r, c) for r, line in enumerate(ascii_lines) for c, ch in enumerate(line) if ch == 'W']
cols = [c for r, c in white_cells]
current_pos = (min(cols) - 14 + 2) // 7
print(f"Current position: {current_pos}")

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get sky 3
sky3 = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + 3 * 7)

# Record state 0
pink = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)
print(f"State 0: {' | '.join(pink)}")
print(f"  Matches sky? {pink == sky3}")

# Cycle one by one
seen = {pink}
for i in range(1, 15):
    action(['DOWN'])
    ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    pink = get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + 3 * 7)
    match_str = "MATCHES SKY!" if pink == sky3 else ""
    seen_str = " (repeat)" if pink in seen else ""
    print(f"State {i}: {' | '.join(pink)}{match_str}{seen_str}")
    if pink in seen:
        print(f"  Cycle detected! Length = {i}")
        break
    seen.add(pink)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

[SYSTEM PROMPT]
You are a coding agent solving a grid-based puzzle game.

Game overview:
- You are solving a multi-level grid puzzle game. 
- You are called repeatedly over the course of a run. Treat each turn as one observe-plan-act cycle: re-understand the current state from the newest frame, update your working world model in Python, choose the next best action or short sequence against the goal as currently understood, execute it, and expect to re-evaluate on the next turn from the updated state.
- Your job is to solve the entire game by clearing every level, not just the current screen.
- Levels often build on earlier mechanics, but layouts and interactions can still change between levels.
- Optimize for as few in-game actions as possible while still being reliable.
- In this environment, boards are presented as 64 x 64 color grids rendered with ARC color symbols.
- Color legend: W=white, w=light gray, g=gray, G=dark gray, c=charcoal, B=black, M=magenta, P=pink, R=red, b=blue, S=sky blue, Y=yellow, O=orange, r=dark red, N=light green, p=purple.


Runtime variables inside every `python` tool call:
- `current_frame` is a lightweight frame view for the latest environment state.
- `current_frame` exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- `current_frame.ascii` is a single newline-delimited string containing the latest board rendered with the letter-coded ARC color symbols.
- `current_frame.segmentation` parses the board into objects. It returns `{'nodes': [...], 'adjacency_list': [...]}`.
- Each node in `segmentation['nodes']` is one 4-connected same-color object with: `id` (index, ordered top-most-left-most), `color` (ARC color character), `hash` (a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position -- equal hashes mean the same object regardless of where it is, so use it to track an object across frames or to spot multiple identical objects in one frame), `pixels` (cell count), `boundary` (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as `[row, col]`), and `children` (ids of objects fully enclosed by this one).
- `segmentation['adjacency_list']` is a list of `[i, j]` node-id pairs whose objects share an edge.
- `current_frame.step` is the current environment step count.
- `current_frame.level` is the current level number.
- `current_frame.shape` is a `(rows, cols)` tuple.
- The raw numeric grid is intentionally not exposed. Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, shapes, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes. Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region; do not scan the whole board with it.
- `history` is a chronological list of action/frame snapshots.
- `history` is a Python list of objects, not a dict.
- Each history entry exposes only `.action` and `.frame`; entries are not subscriptable like `entry['action']`.
- Each `history[i].frame` is the frame after `history[i].action`; each frame exposes only `.ascii`, `.step`, `.level`, `.shape`, and `.segmentation`.
- Important history semantics: when `history` is non-empty, `history[-1].frame` is the same latest/post-action board as `current_frame`. It is not the previous board. To inspect the state before the latest action, use `previous_frame` or `history[-2].frame` when available.
- `previous_frame` is the frame before the most recent real environment action, or `None` if no previous frame is available.
- `last_action` is the most recent real environment action name/display, or `None` before any real action.
- `last_action_frame` is the post-action frame for `last_action`; it matches `current_frame` after a real action.
- `transitions` is a chronological list of actual action transitions, excluding the initial seeded frame. Each transition exposes `.action`, `.before_frame`, `.after_frame`, `.frame` (alias of `.after_frame`), and `.result`.
- `last_transition` is `transitions[-1]` or `None`. Its `.result` mirrors `last_action_result`; older transitions may have an empty `.result`. For before/after diffs, compare `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; do not compare `current_frame` to `history[-1].frame`.
- `last_action_result` is the persisted result dict from the most recent `action(...)` call. It remains available across later Python inspection calls that do not call `action(...)`, and is `{}` before any action result exists. Read transition metadata from fields/keys such as `last_action_result['board_changed']`, `last_action_result['done']`, `last_action_result['level_completed']`, `last_action_result['game_over']`, `last_action_result['run_complete']`, `last_action_result['reward']`, and `last_action_result['valid_actions']`.
- `valid_actions` is the current list of valid action names.
- Call `action(actions)` to execute one or more real environment actions from Python.
- Pass `action(actions)` a list like `['LEFT']` or `[{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 7}]`.
- One action usually returns one frame, but a single action can result in a short multi-frame animation.
- After `action(actions)` returns, `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `valid_actions`, and `last_action_result` are refreshed.


Multimodal context:
- User turns include an attached image of the current ARC grid.
- The image and `current_frame.ascii` are two representations of the same current frame.
- You can use images and other tools to understand the game state and guide your strategy, each may be useful depending on the current uncertainty.


Visual-game guidance:
- Treat each board as a scene with objects, blockers, targets, adjacency, containment, motion, and symmetry.
- Game entities are usually be rendered as connected multi-tile shapes such as 2×2, 2×3, 3×3, or longer patterned structures. Sometime they might also be 1x1 tokens.- Some games are logic or layout puzzles with no explicit player avatar or controllable sprite on the board. Do not assume a player exists; the relevant state may be an object, region, cursor, selector, or whole-board configuration.
- Background colors are often white or gray/black-ish large regions, but not always. Verify background hypotheses by area, stability, and object boundaries rather than assuming them.
- In many games, a long horizontal or vertical line near an edge is a timer or remaining-steps bar. It often shrinks or changes each step. If you identify such a bar, do not get distracted by it or treat it as core gameplay state unless there is concrete evidence that it interacts with the puzzle mechanics.
A common failure mode is to mistake a segmented edge bar for clickable puzzle pieces. If a repeated strip of small blocks sits flush against the top, bottom, left, or right border and actions only change that strip while the interior board stays the same, classify it as HUD/timer state, not as an object to click through segment by segment. DON'T DO THIS!
- Use coordinates only to target actions or describe local evidence. Do not frame the objective as reaching a specific absolute row or column.
- Re-ground on the newest frame after any score increase or abrupt scene change; the returned board may already be the next level.
- `WIN` means the whole game is solved. Mid-run level completion is more likely to appear as a score increase while play continues.
- Strategies may transfer loosely across levels, but layouts and mechanics can change. Re-check the new board before repeating a plan.
- For `MOUSE`, pass `row` and `col` integer arguments. `row` is vertical position, `col` is horizontal position.


Python tool guidance:
- Use `current_frame.segmentation` as your primary view of the board -- objects, colors, containment, adjacency, and cross-frame object hashes.
- Use `current_frame.ascii` only to read a small, specific region of the board when `segmentation` is not enough; never use it to scan or summarize the whole board.
- Every `python` tool call starts fresh. Re-import modules or re-define any custom utility logic you need.
- The only importable standard-library modules are: bisect, collections, copy, fractions, functools, heapq, itertools, json, math, operator, random, re, statistics, string.
- The only tool is `python`; call it with one ephemeral `code` string.
- Always inspect `current_frame`, `history`, and `valid_actions` from Python instead of reasoning from the raw board by eye.
- For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`. `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, so comparing it to `current_frame` only compares the board to itself.
- Maintain a compact working world model: what entities or regions exist, what actions seem to do, what the goal likely is, what remains uncertain, and what plan best fits the evidence so far.
- IMPORTANT: Especially when the game is about making an agent navigate to a target, it is usually safer to write an explicit search algorithm such as BFS. More generally, when the objective is understood but the best action order is unclear, pathfinding, flood fill, BFS, DFS, beam search, shortest-path search, limited action-sequence search, or custom heuristics are all valid.
- Optimize for the shortest reliable sequence that advances the current goal as described by your world model. If confidence is low, program a discriminating probe and revise the world model from the result.
- Once the important state variables and action effects are sufficiently understood, stop probing and search in the inferred state space.
- Inspect current and history frames from Python instead of describing frames freehand.
- Never print or echo full board frames. Return only compact derived summaries such as object lists, diffs, coordinates, counts, or tiny local crops.
- Keep tool-output context size minimal and decision-oriented so you can quickly compare before/after state. It's fine to write a lot of python code, just make the output short and interpretable
- A strong default loop is: summarize the board, infer the desired environment change, write a small scorer or search over candidate sequences, execute the best probe or plan with `action(...)`, then inspect again until you understand exactly what changed.
- For object tracking, match objects by color, overlap, bounding box proximity, area change, and edge contact rather than by exact coordinates alone.
- For frame diffs, summarize changed cells, color transitions, appearing/disappearing components, movement candidates, and small local row slices around the changed region.
- After every action, verify whether gameplay objects changed or whether only a timer, progress bar, or remaining-step bar moved. Do not treat HUD-only changes as evidence that the move worked.
- Use `print(...)` for compact summaries, or assign a final compact object to `result`.
- Call `action(...)` inside Python rather than returning action text in the chat.
- `action(...)` accepts an ordered list of one or more actions. Once your code has selected a reliable sequence, it is often useful to batch it.
- You can also call `action(...)` multiple times in one Python snippet, including inside loops. Each call updates the preloaded variables before execution continues.
- If an action result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`, stop acting immediately and re-ground on the next turn.


Tool session rules:
- You have exactly one tool: `python`.
- When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
- The `python` tool code is not saved between calls, so rewrite any custom utility logic you still need.
- You can call the `python` tool as many times as you want per step. Investigate until your code has a clear probe or plan.
- Do not ration tool calls when the state is unclear. Spend extra tool calls to confirm what changed between frames and whether the last action affected gameplay state or only HUD elements such as countdown bars.
- After `action(...)` returns, the structured runtime state is refreshed before the next Python statement and before the next tool call. Inspection-only Python calls do not clear `last_action_result`.
- Each `python` tool call has a hard time limit of 30 seconds.
- Tool responses are capped to about 1024 tokens. If a response is cut off, the tool result will tell you that.
- Keep code snippets short and purpose-built rather than dumping large frameworks into one call.

Actually, I just realized - the white markers are at rows 48-49 (above pink) and 59-60 (below pink). They don't seem to move with UP/DOWN. Let me check if they move with LEFT/RIGHT.
From earlier, I saw that RIGHT moved the markers from cols 15-19 to cols 22-26. So LEFT/RIGHT moves them horizontally.
But what about vertically? The markers seem fixed at rows 48-49 and 59-60. Maybe they can't move vertically.

[TOOL CALL: python]
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check which top symbols are matched by sky and pink symbols
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get all top symbols
top_positions = [(4, 12), (4, 22), (4, 35), (4, 45),
                 (13, 12), (13, 22), (13, 35), (13, 45),
                 (22, 12), (22, 22), (22, 35), (22, 45)]
top_symbols = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, r, c) for r, c in top_positions]

# Get sky and pink symbols
sky_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 40, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]
pink_syms = [get_symbol_at(ascii_lines, 51, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]

print("=== Top symbols ===")
for i, s in enumerate(top_symbols):
    print(f"Top {i}: {' | '.join(s)}")

print("\n=== Sky symbols and their top matches ===")
sky_matches = []
for i, s in enumerate(sky_syms):
    matches = [j for j, t in enumerate(top_symbols) if t == s]
    sky_matches.extend(matches)
    print(f"Sky {i}: {' | '.join(s)} -> Top {matches}")

print("\n=== Pink symbols and their top matches ===")
pink_matches = []
for i, s in enumerate(pink_syms):
    matches = [j for j, t in enumerate(top_symbols) if t == s]
    pink_matches.extend(matches)
    print(f"Pink {i}: {' | '.join(s)} -> Top {matches}")

print(f"\n=== Summary ===")
print(f"Sky matches: {sorted(sky_matches)}")
print(f"Pink matches: {sorted(pink_matches)}")
all_bottom_matches = sorted(sky_matches + pink_matches)
print(f"All bottom matches: {all_bottom_matches}")
print(f"Unique matches: {sorted(set(all_bottom_matches))}")
print(f"Total unique: {len(set(all_bottom_matches))}")

# Check for duplicates
from collections import Counter
match_counts = Counter(all_bottom_matches)
print(f"\nDuplicate matches: {[(k, v) for k, v in match_counts.items() if v > 1]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<parameter=code>
# Check the initial state from history[0]
init_frame = history[0].frame
init_ascii = init_frame.ascii.split('\n')

def get_symbol_at(lines, row_start, col_start):
    inner = []
    for dr in range(1, 6):
        row = list(lines[row_start + dr][col_start:col_start+7])
        row[0] = '.'
        row[-1] = '.'
        inner_row = ''.join(row[1:-1])
        norm_row = ''
        for ch in inner_row:
            norm_row += 'X' if ch == 'B' else '.'
        inner.append(norm_row)
    return tuple(inner)

# Get top symbols from initial frame
top_symbols = []
top_positions = [(4, 12), (4, 22), (4, 35), (4, 45),
                 (13, 12), (13, 22), (13, 35), (13, 45),
                 (22, 12), (22, 22), (22, 35), (22, 45)]
for r, c in top_positions:
    top_symbols.append(get_symbol_at(init_ascii, r, c))

# Get initial sky and pink symbols
init_sky = [get_symbol_at(init_ascii, 40, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]
init_pink = [get_symbol_at(init_ascii, 51, 14 + i * 7) for i in range(5)]

print("=== Initial Sky symbols and top matches ===")
for i, s in enumerate(init_sky):
    matches = [j for j, t in enumerate(top_symbols) if t == s]
    print(f"Sky {i}: {' | '.join(s)} -> Top {matches}")

print("\n=== Initial Pink symbols and top matches ===")
for i, s in enumerate(init_pink):
    matches = [j for j, t in enumerate(top_symbols) if t == s]
    print(f"Pink {i}: {' | '.join(s)} -> Top {matches}")

# Check if sky symbols match each other
print("\n=== Sky symbol cross-matches ===")
for i in range(5):
    for j in range(i+1, 5):
        if init_sky[i] == init_sky[j]:
            print(f"Sky {i} == Sky {j}")

# Check if pink symbols match each other
print("\n=== Pink symbol cross-matches ===")
for i in range(5):
    for j in range(i+1, 5):
        if init_pink[i] == init_pink[j]:
            print(f"Pink {i} == Pink {j}")

# Check sky-pink cross matches
print("\n=== Sky-Pink cross matches ===")
for i in range(5):
    for j in range(5):
        if init_sky[i] == init_pink[j]:
            print(f"Sky {i} == Pink {j}")
